title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
A 34-year-old woman is in the trauma bay after a high-speed crash. Alert, oriented, full capacity. CT shows a grade IV splenic laceration; her hemoglobin has fallen from 13 to 7 in the last hour. The surgeon wants her in the OR, with pre-emptive transfusion. Tucked in her wallet is a signed, dated card declaring her a Jehovah’s Witness and refusing all blood products. Her parents have just arrived and are pleading with the team to “do whatever it takes.”
You are the nurse at her bedside.

What questions would you want answered before doing anything?
Who exactly is asking you to do what?
What do you think the right move is — and why?











Note



The legal frame. U.S. courts have, in nearly every case involving a competent adult, upheld the patient’s right to refuse blood — even when refusal is fatal. The harder cases involve minors, pregnancy, and unclear capacity. We will come back to all three later in the course.



Let students sit with this. We’ll come back to it after we have vocabulary. The whole rest of the lecture is, in a sense, equipping us to answer this question more carefully than “follow your gut.”





By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Define bioethics and explain why it emerged as a distinct field
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, and personal-preference claims
Name and apply the four principles of biomedical ethics
Identify the premises and conclusion of a moral argument written in ordinary prose
Recognize a few common fallacies in bioethical reasoning

title: Introduction to Bioethics
text:
0.2 What Is Bioethics?
Bioethics is the systematic study of ethical questions arising from:

Advances in medicine and medical technology
Biological research and biotechnology
Healthcare policy and resource allocation
The human relationship with living organisms and the environment


Speaker notes: Introduce the field and why it has grown in importance since the mid-20th century. Mention the Nuremberg Code and Tuskegee as historical catalysts.

title: Introduction to Bioethics
text:
0.3 Core Ethical Frameworks


Consequentialism

Judges actions by outcomes
“The greatest good for the greatest number”
Key figure: John Stuart Mill


Deontology

Judges actions by duties and rules
Rights are inviolable regardless of outcomes
Key figure: Immanuel Kant

title: Introduction to Bioethics
text:
1.1 The Four Principles of Bioethics

Autonomy — respect for the patient’s right to make informed decisions
Beneficence — act in the patient’s best interest
Non-maleficence — “first, do no harm”
Justice — fair distribution of benefits and burdens







Note



These four principles form the foundation of most clinical ethics reasoning in the United States and much of the world (Beauchamp and Childress 2019).

title: Introduction to Bioethics
text:
1.4 A First Question

A 70-year-old patient with advanced cancer refuses chemotherapy that her oncologist believes could extend her life by a year. Her adult children beg the team to convince her otherwise.
Which of the four principles are in tension here, and how would you weigh them?

title: Introduction to Bioethics
text:
1.5 Wrapping Up

Bioethics asks how moral principles apply to medicine, research, and the life sciences. We use a four-principle framework as our common vocabulary, and we’ll see how consequentialist and deontological reasoning illuminate (and sometimes conflict over) that framework throughout the course.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
This course consists of 12 lectures, each delivered in two parts (designed for a twice-weekly schedule). Online students watch both parts as a single unit. Alongside the lectures, students read and discuss 12 case study documents drawn from historical and contemporary bioethics.
The course is organized around the four principles of biomedical ethics — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice. Major ethical theories (utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, care ethics, principlism) are introduced as lenses that ground and sometimes conflict over these principles.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
2 Course-Level Learning Outcomes
By the end of the course, students will be able to:

Identify and apply the four principles of biomedical ethics (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to clinical and research scenarios.
Analyze moral arguments — identifying premises, conclusions, hidden assumptions, and common fallacies.
Compare how major ethical frameworks (utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, care, principlist) approach the same dilemma and yield different conclusions.
Evaluate real and contemporary bioethics cases using appropriate legal, historical, and empirical context.
Articulate their own reasoned position on a contested bioethical issue, engaging fairly with opposing views.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
4 Lecture-Level Learning Outcomes

4.1 L1. Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning

Define the four principles of biomedical ethics and give a clinical example of each.
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, or personal-preference claims.
Identify the premises and conclusion of a moral argument written in ordinary prose.
Recognize common fallacies in bioethical reasoning (ad hominem, appeal to nature, slippery slope, naturalistic fallacy).
Summarize the core commitments of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics.
Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently.



4.2 L2. History & the Clinical Relationship

Trace key events in the development of modern bioethics (Hippocratic oath, Nazi experiments, Nuremberg Code, Tuskegee, Belmont Report).
Compare the four Emanuel & Emanuel models of the clinician-patient relationship (paternalistic, informative, interpretive, deliberative).
Explain the four elements of valid informed consent: disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, and decision-making capacity.
Analyze conflicts between patient autonomy and clinician beneficence.
Identify legal and ethical limits of confidentiality, including Tarasoff duty-to-warn and mandatory reporting.
Discuss how cultural context complicates truth-telling norms.



4.3 L3. Research Ethics

Explain the basic structure of a randomized controlled trial (randomization, blinding, placebo, control group, intention-to-treat).
Distinguish research from clinical care and describe the “therapeutic misconception.”
Summarize lessons from Tuskegee, Willowbrook, and Nazi medical experiments.
Apply the Belmont principles (respect for persons, beneficence, justice) to IRB review.
Evaluate the ethics of placebo-controlled trials when effective treatment exists.
Identify vulnerable populations and the heightened protections they require.
Analyze ethical tensions in conducting trials in low-resource settings (e.g., short-course AZT trials).



4.4 L4. Abortion

Describe the post-Dobbs legal landscape and variation across US states.
Explain Thomson’s violinist argument and Marquis’s future-like-ours argument.
Evaluate criteria for moral status (biological humanity, personhood, sentience, viability).
Analyze reproductive justice and feminist perspectives on bodily autonomy.
Apply the four principles to specific cases (rape, fetal anomaly, maternal life, gestational age).
Assess how real-world clinical implementation of exceptions succeeds or fails.



4.5 L5. Reproductive Technology

Describe IVF, ICSI, PGD/PGT, and gestational surrogacy at a functional level.
Evaluate arguments for and against commercial surrogacy and egg donation.
Analyze the commodification critique (Anderson) and the procreative liberty response (Robertson).
Discuss “savior siblings,” posthumous reproduction, and synthetic gametes.
Apply the four principles to embryo selection cases (sex selection, tissue matching, disability).



4.6 L6. Genetics & Enhancement

Explain core genetics concepts: gene, genome, allele, germline vs. somatic cells, inheritance patterns.
Describe CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing in accessible terms.
Distinguish gene therapy from genetic enhancement and assess whether the line is morally meaningful.
Analyze the He Jiankui (2018) case and the global response to germline editing.
Explain the non-identity problem and the “child’s right to an open future” argument.
Evaluate arguments for and against enhancement (Savulescu, Sandel, Glannon).



4.7 L7. End of Life

Distinguish brain death, coma, persistent vegetative state, and minimally conscious state.
Explain advance directives, living wills, durable power of attorney, and POLST/MOLST forms.
Compare active euthanasia, passive euthanasia, withdrawal of treatment, and physician-assisted death.
Evaluate Rachels’ argument that active and passive euthanasia are morally equivalent.
Analyze surrogate decision-making standards (substituted judgment vs. best interest).
Discuss disability community critiques of MAID and the controversy over psychiatric MAID.



4.8 L8. Justice & Health Care Allocation

Compare utilitarian, Rawlsian, libertarian, and capabilities theories of justice.
Evaluate arguments for and against a moral right to health care.
Compare US, Canadian, German, and UK health systems on key features (coverage, financing, outcomes).
Explain documented racial and socioeconomic health disparities and their mechanisms.
Analyze implicit bias in clinical encounters and its effects on care.
Discuss ethical frameworks for organ allocation and rationing.



4.9 L9. Public Health Ethics

Distinguish clinical ethics from public health ethics (different unit of concern, different trade-offs).
Analyze tensions between individual liberty and collective welfare in quarantine and vaccine mandates.
Evaluate arguments for mandatory vaccination and conscientious exemptions.
Apply crisis standards of care to pandemic triage scenarios.
Discuss what COVID-19 revealed about existing inequities and governance failures.



4.10 L10. AI in Medicine

Explain at a basic level how machine learning diagnostic and predictive tools are built and deployed.
Identify sources of algorithmic bias (training data, proxy variables, deployment drift).
Analyze the Optum care-management algorithm case and the Epic Sepsis Model controversy.
Evaluate informed consent when clinicians rely on black-box AI recommendations.
Discuss AI mental-health chatbots and their ethical risks.
Consider liability and professional responsibility when AI is in the clinical loop.
Examine data-privacy and consent issues specific to health AI.



4.11 L11. Gender, Identity & Disability

Describe current gender-affirming care pathways for adults and for adolescents.
Summarize the Cass Review and the contested reception of its evidentiary standards.
Analyze parental authority, adolescent assent, and clinical gatekeeping in pediatric gender care.
Distinguish “mere difference” and “bad difference” views of disability (Barnes vs. Bognar).
Evaluate the disability-rights critique of selective abortion (Asch).
Discuss body integrity identity disorder and the limits of patient autonomy.
Apply the four principles to cases at the intersection of identity and medicine.



4.12 L12. Animal Ethics

Compare major views on animal moral status (Kantian, Singer’s utilitarian, Regan’s rights view).
Evaluate the 3 Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement) as an ethical framework for animal research.
Discuss primate research and the use of animal models for neurological and psychiatric disorders.
Analyze the public-health and welfare ethics of industrial animal agriculture.
Evaluate xenotransplantation in light of recent pig-heart and pig-kidney clinical trials.
Apply moral-status arguments developed in earlier units (abortion, genetics, end-of-life) to animal cases.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Who: Donald “Dax” Cowart (1947–2019), a 25-year-old Air Force pilot from Texas
What: Severely burned in a propane explosion in 1973; demanded treatment be stopped throughout two years of intensely painful care; was overruled and treated against his wishes
Where / When: Parkland Hospital (Dallas) and the University of Texas Medical Branch (Galveston), 1973–1975
Why it matters: Cowart’s case became the most-discussed example in American bioethics of the conflict between patient autonomy (the right to refuse treatment) and clinical beneficence (the duty to save a life). Even after he recovered and built a successful career, Cowart insisted his rights had been violated.
Pair with: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
The Case
In July 1973, Dax Cowart and his father, Ray, drove to a piece of rural land in east Texas that Ray was thinking of buying. A propane line under the property had ruptured, and the gas had pooled invisibly in a low area near where they parked. When Ray turned the car’s ignition to leave, the spark detonated the gas. Ray died three months later from his injuries. Dax, then 25, survived — but burns covered roughly 65% of his body, including deep (third-degree) burns to his face, hands, and upper body. He was blinded in both eyes, lost most of his fingers, and lost both ears.
He was conscious throughout. According to multiple accounts, the first words he spoke to a passing farmer at the scene were a request to be shot.
He was admitted first to Parkland Memorial Hospital in Dallas, then transferred to the burn unit at the University of Texas Medical Branch in Galveston, where he would spend most of the next 14 months. The standard treatment for burns of this severity at that time — and largely still today — required daily debridement: the removal of dead and infected tissue to allow new skin to grow and to prevent fatal sepsis. At UTMB, debridement was performed in a Hubbard tank, a stainless-steel pool of warm chlorinated water in which the patient’s burned tissue was scrubbed away. Patients commonly described the procedure as the worst pain of their lives.
From very early in his treatment, Cowart asked that the tankings be stopped, that no further skin grafts be performed, and that he be allowed to die. He asked his physicians, his nurses, and his mother. He asked repeatedly, persistently, and — psychiatrists who evaluated him concluded — competently. He understood his prognosis. He understood that refusing treatment would mean a death from infection within weeks. He preferred that to continuing.
His treating physicians — most prominently Dr. Charles Baxter, a pioneering burn specialist — refused. Cowart’s mother, Ada, a devout Baptist, served as his legal surrogate and authorized the continuation of treatment. The team accepted her authorization. For two years, he was forcibly medicated, restrained when necessary, and treated against his stated wishes.
He survived. He returned home blind, severely disabled, and dependent on others for many basic tasks. He attempted suicide twice. He eventually went to law school, earned his J.D. from Texas Tech in 1986, married, divorced, and built a career as a practicing attorney and public speaker.
For the rest of his life, Cowart said two things that puzzled many listeners. First: he was glad to be alive. Second: his physicians had been wrong to keep him alive against his will. The two statements, he insisted, did not contradict each other. The question, for him, was never whether the outcome was good — it was whether the choice had been his to make.
He died in 2019.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Context Students Need

Burn injury severity. Burns are classified by depth. First-degree burns affect only the outer skin layer (sunburn). Second-degree burns reach into deeper layers and blister. Third-degree (full-thickness) burns destroy all skin layers and the nerve endings underneath. Counterintuitively, the deepest burns are often not the most painful — but the surrounding partial-thickness burns are. Cowart’s burns covered roughly 65% of his “total body surface area” (TBSA), with much of it third-degree. In 1973, mortality at that severity exceeded 50%; survival required months of grafting and debridement.


Debridement and the Hubbard tank. New skin will not grow over dead tissue, and dead tissue invites infection that can kill within days. Debridement — physically removing that tissue — is essential. The Hubbard tank used at UTMB allowed warm water and saline to soften the eschar (dead skin) so that nurses could scrub it off. The procedure is shorter and less brutal today, with better anesthesia and surgical alternatives, but the underlying necessity remains.


Informed consent in 1973. The legal doctrine of informed consent was very new. Canterbury v. Spence, the federal case that established the modern “reasonable patient” standard for disclosure, had been decided just one year earlier (1972). Most physicians of Dr. Baxter’s generation had been trained in a model where the doctor decided what was best and the patient cooperated. Patient refusal of life-saving treatment was treated, by default, as evidence that the patient was not thinking clearly. (For the long arc by which informed consent emerged as a doctrine, see Faden and Beauchamp (1986).)


Decision-making capacity. “Capacity” is a clinical term distinct from “competence” (a legal term). A patient has capacity to make a specific decision if they can (1) understand the relevant information, (2) appreciate how it applies to their situation, (3) reason about treatment options, and (4) communicate a choice. Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific — a patient may have capacity to refuse one treatment but not another, or capacity today but not tomorrow. Multiple psychiatrists evaluated Cowart and found that he had capacity.


Surrogate decision-making. When a patient lacks capacity, a surrogate (often a family member) decides on their behalf. The American standard hierarchy is substituted judgment (decide what the patient would have wanted) followed by best interest (decide what serves the patient’s welfare). Critically: surrogates have no authority to override a patient who does have capacity. In Cowart’s case, his mother’s religious convictions were given controlling weight even though Cowart himself was found capable of deciding.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Ethical Issues
The case is usually framed as a collision between two of the four principles of biomedical ethics:
Autonomy. Cowart was a competent adult. He understood his condition and his prognosis. He chose, repeatedly and consistently, to refuse treatment. The standard view in modern bioethics is that competent patients have an essentially unconditional right to refuse any medical intervention, including life-saving care. On this view, his treatment team violated his rights, full stop.
Beneficence and non-maleficence. Cowart’s physicians believed — correctly, as it turned out — that he could survive and eventually rebuild a meaningful life. They saw their refusal to stop treatment as an act of mercy and skill, not coercion. From their perspective, allowing a patient in extremis to make an irreversible decision that he might later regret was a failure of medical responsibility, not a respect for it.
Justice and the role of family. The team’s deference to Cowart’s mother raises a different question: under what circumstances should family preferences override a competent patient’s expressed wishes? The case shows how easily institutional power can route around a patient’s voice when family and medical authority align.
A subtler issue runs underneath all of this: the problem of retrospective endorsement. Cowart’s later statement that he was glad to be alive is sometimes invoked as a justification for what was done to him. (“See? He thanks us now.”) Cowart himself rejected this argument. The fact that an outcome turned out well does not, he argued, retroactively legitimize a decision that was not the patient’s to make. A surgeon who operates on the wrong leg cannot defend the act by pointing out that the patient walked again.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Multiple Perspectives
Dax Cowart himself insisted throughout his life that the question was about control over his own body, not about outcome. “I do not want to die because the quality of life is too low,” he told an interviewer in 1989. “I want to die because the quality of life is too low and I am the one who has to live it.” His position was libertarian about autonomy in the strong sense: a competent person’s right to refuse extends even to refusals others find tragic or mistaken.
The treating physicians, particularly Dr. Charles Baxter, defended their decisions in interviews and in the films made about the case. Baxter argued that severely burned patients in acute pain frequently ask to die early in treatment and almost universally change their minds once they have recovered. To honor the early refusal, on this view, would be to mistake an episode of suffering for a settled preference.
Ada Cowart, Dax’s mother, was a religious woman who believed life was a gift from God that her son had no right to refuse. She authorized continued treatment as both a parent and a Christian. She lived to see her son recover; her authorization is, in her own framework, vindicated.
Philosophers and legal scholars have used the case to develop competing accounts. Robert Burt, in a well-known dialogue with Cowart (Cowart and Burt 1998), argued that the relationship between physician and suffering patient cannot be modeled as a simple consent transaction — that the demand to die was, in part, a demand to be heard, and that the right response was not always to comply with the literal request. Cowart pushed back hard.
Disability rights advocates have noted that Cowart’s case prefigures contemporary debates about medical assistance in dying. They observe a recurring asymmetry: when an able-bodied person refuses care, clinicians often comply; when a newly disabled person refuses care, clinicians often resist. The implicit assumption — that life with disability is intolerable, but that this assumption can be overridden by treatment — is, they argue, exactly backwards.

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’
text:
Discussion Questions

Was Dax Cowart’s refusal of treatment a refusal that his physicians should have honored? Defend your answer using at least one of the four principles.
Cowart later said he was glad to be alive and that his rights had been violated. Are these two claims consistent? What does your answer imply about how we should evaluate paternalistic decisions in retrospect?
Cowart’s mother authorized continued treatment as his legal surrogate. Under modern standards of decision-making capacity, was her authorization valid? Should it have been given any weight at all if Cowart was competent?
Dr. Baxter argued that severely burned patients almost always change their minds about wanting to die after they recover. Suppose this is statistically true. Does it justify overriding any individual patient’s refusal? Why or why not?
Compare Cowart’s case to a contemporary patient who requests medical aid in dying (MAID) for a terminal illness. What features of the cases are similar, and what features are different?
Imagine you are a nurse in the burn unit assigned to perform the daily tankings. Cowart has just told you, again, that he wants to stop and be allowed to die. What do you do, and what do you say? What do you owe him in this moment that the institution does not owe him?
Some disability rights scholars argue that clinicians systematically underestimate the future quality of life of newly disabled patients. If true, how should that empirical fact change the ethical analysis of Cowart’s case?

title: Case 1 — Dax Cowart: ‘Please Let Me Die’

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
A 34-year-old woman is in the trauma bay after a high-speed crash. Alert, oriented, full capacity. CT shows a grade IV splenic laceration; her hemoglobin has fallen from 13 to 7 in the last hour. The surgeon wants her in the OR, with pre-emptive transfusion. Tucked in her wallet is a signed, dated card declaring her a Jehovah’s Witness and refusing all blood products. Her parents have just arrived and are pleading with the team to “do whatever it takes.”
You are the nurse at her bedside.

What questions would you want answered before doing anything?
Who exactly is asking you to do what?
What do you think the right move is — and why?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.1 A Working Definition
Bioethics is the systematic study of ethical questions arising in:

The care of patients (clinical ethics)
Biomedical research on humans and animals (research ethics)
Public health policy and the allocation of scarce resources
New biotechnologies — genetics, AI, reproductive technology


“Systematic” matters. Bioethics isn’t just having opinions — it’s reasoning about them in a way others can examine, challenge, and refine.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.2 Why a Distinct Field?
A short list of mid-20th-century shocks:

The Nazi medical experiments exposed at Nuremberg (1945–1947)
The Tuskegee syphilis study (1932–1972), in which Black men were left untreated for decades
New technology that outran old norms: ventilators, dialysis, organ transplants, IVF
Patients and families demanding a voice the old paternalistic model didn’t give them

Bioethics is what grew up to answer the question: how should we govern this?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.3 Three Sample Questions


Clinical: Can a 16-year-old refuse chemotherapy her parents want her to have?
Research: Is it ethical to give half the participants a placebo when an effective drug already exists?
Policy: When ICU beds run out in a pandemic, who gets one?



Each of these is a recurring exam case. Same field, very different scales — bedside, study, society.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Why the Distinctions Matter
Students often collapse these. They are not the same — and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in bioethical reasoning.








Type of claim
Source of authority
Question it answers




Ethical
Reasons we can examine and share
What ought we to do?


Legal
Statutes, courts, regulators
What may or must we do under law?


Religious
Sacred texts, traditions, communities
What does this faith require?


Preference
Personal taste
What do I happen to want?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 Ethics vs. Law
The law is not a reliable guide to what is ethical.

Slavery in the United States was legal until 1865 — and was monstrously unethical the entire time.
The Tuskegee study was legal in 1932. It was wrong in 1932.
Conversely, jaywalking is illegal in most cities; almost no one thinks it is a moral failing.







Tip



A useful test for nurses: “Is it legal?” and “Is it the right thing for this patient?” are two different questions. Sometimes the law lags behind ethics; sometimes it overreaches.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.3 Ethics vs. Religion
Many of our patients — and many of you — draw moral guidance from religious tradition. That is appropriate. But:

A religious tradition can give its members reasons to act, but cannot, on its own, give non-members reasons to act.
Bioethical arguments need to work in a pluralistic society where patients, families, and clinicians come from many traditions and none.

We will treat religious commitments as important data about what patients value — not as conversation-stoppers in clinical reasoning.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.4 Ethics vs. Personal Preference

“I just don’t think it’s right.”

That is a feeling, not yet an argument. Ethical reasoning asks the next question: why? What reason, that another reasonable person could weigh, supports the conclusion?

This is the moment to flag that “I feel” / “I believe” are perfectly fine starting points — but bioethics asks us to take the next step.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.5 Thought Question

A nurse on the L&D floor is asked to assist with a second-trimester abortion for a patient whose fetus has a lethal anomaly. The procedure is legal; her own physician is performing it. The nurse declines on religious grounds and asks to be reassigned. Her hospital has a conscience clause — a policy permitting clinicians to opt out of specific procedures — and she has invoked it formally.

Is what she is doing legal? Ethical? Religiously required? A personal preference?
Could the answers come apart? In what scenario?
Now imagine the same refusal at 3 a.m. at a small rural hospital with no other nurse on shift. Anything change?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.1 A Brief Origin Story
In the wake of Tuskegee, a U.S. national commission produced the Belmont Report (1979) (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979). That same year, philosophers Tom Beauchamp and James Childress published Principles of Biomedical Ethics (Beauchamp and Childress 2019), proposing four principles meant to be:

Shared across ethical traditions
Specific enough to guide clinical decisions
General enough to apply to new technologies

The result is what’s often called principlism — and it remains the lingua franca of hospital ethics consults (Jonsen 1998).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.2 1. Autonomy
Autonomy — self-rule. Respect the patient’s right to make informed decisions about her own body and care.
In practice, autonomy underwrites:

Informed consent
The right to refuse treatment (even life-saving treatment)
Truth-telling and honest disclosure
Confidentiality


The Jehovah’s Witness case from the start of class is, at its core, an autonomy case. Hold that thought.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.4 2. Beneficence
Beneficence — do good. Act in the patient’s best interest; promote welfare.
This sounds obvious until you ask: whose account of the patient’s good?

The clinician’s professional judgment?
The patient’s own values?
The family’s hopes?

Beneficence and autonomy can pull in opposite directions.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.6 3. Non-Maleficence
Non-maleficence — first, do no harm (primum non nocere).


The oldest principle in Western medicine, traceable to the Hippocratic tradition (5th c. BCE). It does not mean “never do anything risky.” It means: weigh the harms, and don’t expose patients to harm without proportionate benefit.
Every chemotherapy regimen, every surgery, every medication is a deliberate small harm in service of a larger good.



Hippocrates, engraving by Peter Paul Rubens. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.8 4. Justice
Justice — fair distribution of benefits and burdens.
In bioethics, justice shows up in three main forms:

Distributive justice — who gets the kidney, the ICU bed, the new drug?
Procedural justice — was the process fair, even if the outcome is hard?
Social justice — are we honest about the historical and structural causes of health disparities?


We’ll spend an entire lecture (L8) on justice. For now, just plant the flag: bioethics is not only about individual encounters.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.10 The Four Together





flowchart LR
  A[Autonomy<br/>respect choices] --- B[Beneficence<br/>do good]
  B --- N[Non-maleficence<br/>do no harm]
  N --- J[Justice<br/>treat fairly]
  J --- A

  style B fill:#fce9d6,stroke:#b07d1a,stroke-width:2px
  style N fill:#fad7d2,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
  style J fill:#dfe6ee,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px






A framework, not an algorithm. The principles tell you what to weigh — not, by themselves, how to weigh it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.11 When Principles Conflict
Most interesting bioethics cases are conflicts between principles, not conflicts between good and evil.

Autonomy vs. Beneficence — the Jehovah’s Witness refusing transfusion
Beneficence vs. Non-Maleficence — risky surgery with a small chance of cure
Autonomy vs. Justice — vaccine refusal during a pandemic
Beneficence vs. Justice — spending heroic resources on one patient when others wait

The four principles do not resolve these tensions. They make them visible so we can argue about them carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.12 Thought Question

Return to our trauma-bay patient — full capacity, signed refusal, parents at the door. Now you have vocabulary for what was hard.

Which principles point toward transfusing her? Which point away?
Which counts as the “harm” — letting her die, or doing something to her body she has explicitly refused?
Which principle would you weight most heavily, and why?
What single fact, if changed, would change your answer?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.1 What Is an Argument?
An argument, in philosophy, is not a quarrel. It is a structured piece of reasoning:

One or more premises (claims offered as support)
A conclusion (the claim the premises are meant to support)

The job of the careful reader: find the conclusion first, then the premises, then ask whether the premises are true and whether they actually support the conclusion.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.2 Worked Example
A short argument you might hear on the floor:

“We have to transfuse her — she’ll die otherwise, and our job is to save lives.”








argument


P1

Premise 1
Without a transfusion,
the patient will die.



C

∴  Conclusion
We should transfuse her.



P1->C





P2

Premise 2
Our job is
to save lives.



P2->C

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.3 Hidden Assumptions
Sounds airtight — until you notice what’s missing. That argument quietly assumes:

“Saving lives” always outweighs the patient’s other wishes.
The patient’s refusal can be set aside because it’s “irrational.”
A nurse’s professional duty is identical to “extending biological life.”

Each of those is contestable. Surfacing hidden assumptions is half the work of bioethics.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.4 A Few Common Fallacies







Fallacy
What it does




Appeal to nature
“It’s natural, so it’s good” — or “unnatural, so bad”


Slippery slope
“If we allow X, eventually Y horrible thing will follow” — without showing the slide


Ad hominem
Attacking the person rather than the argument


Naturalistic fallacy
Inferring ought directly from is


False dichotomy
“Either X or Y” — when other options exist

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.7 Practice — Spot the Fallacy

Identify the fallacy in each:

“Gene editing is wrong — it’s playing God.”
“If we let doctors help patients die, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”
“You can’t trust her opinion on vaccines — she’s not even a real scientist.”
“Either we ration care by ability to pay, or the system collapses.”



Answers (in order): appeal to nature, slippery slope, ad hominem, false dichotomy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.8 Wrapping Up Part A

Bioethics is the systematic study of moral questions in medicine, research, and policy. It is not the same as law, religion, or personal preference. The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary for the conflicts we’ll see all semester. And every claim in this course will be evaluated as an argument: premises, conclusion, hidden assumptions, possible fallacies.
In Part B, we ask the next question: where do these principles come from? We meet the major ethical theories — utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, and care — that ground and sometimes contest the four principles.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.3 Autonomy in Practice

Autonomy self-rule


The case. A 28-year-old with type-1 diabetes (A1c 11.2) declines an insulin pump for the third time. She is tired of being told what to do.
What respecting autonomy looks like here:

Make sure she understands the risks — disclosure
Assess decision-making capacity (she has it)
Document clearly; continue to offer support without coercion

Autonomy is the floor. It does not require us to abandon her — only not to override her.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.5 Beneficence in Practice

Beneficence do good


The case. An 82-year-old with end-stage heart failure, fifth admission this year. The team recommends comfort-focused care; he wants “everything done.” His daughter asks the team to “talk him out of it.”
Whose idea of his good?

The team’s clinical judgment about prognosis?
His own stated wishes, formed over a long life?
His daughter’s fear of losing him?

Beneficence is not the same as paternalism. It is the discipline of finding out what this person counts as a life worth living — and acting in service of that.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.7 Non-Maleficence in Practice

Non-Maleficence do no harm


The case. A new chemotherapy regimen offers a 30% chance of remission and a 5% chance of fatal cardiotoxicity. Standard care offers neither.
The principle does not say “never harm.” It asks:

Is the expected harm proportionate to the expected benefit?
Does the patient understand both sides clearly enough to choose?

Every IV start is a small puncture wound. Every medication has side effects. The principle’s job is to forbid hidden, gratuitous, or disproportionate risk — not all risk.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.9 Justice in Practice

Justice treat fairly


The case. Two 55-year-olds arrive in the ED with chest pain and no obvious red flags. One is on Medicaid; the other has private insurance. Decades of research show that, on average, they will not get the same workup.
Justice at the bedside is rarely a kidney allocation decision. It looks like:

Are we ordering tests for clinical reasons — or for who the patient appears to be?
Are our floors and ICUs staffed and supplied equitably?
Where do we, as nurses, push back against disparate care?

Justice asks us to look up from the individual patient and notice the pattern.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.4 What Is a Fallacy?
A fallacy is an error in reasoning — an argument that looks like good support but isn’t.

Not the same as a lie. The speaker may sincerely believe it.
A fallacious argument can still have a true conclusion — the defect is in the structure, not the conclusion.
Most bioethics fallacies are informal: the form looks fine, but the content misleads through irrelevance, ambiguity, or hidden assumption.

The next two slides cover the six you will see most often.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.5 Common Fallacies in Bioethics


Appeal to Nature Inferring that something is good (or bad) merely because it is natural (or unnatural). “IVF lets older women conceive — but it’s unnatural, so it must be wrong.”


Slippery Slope Predicting that allowing X will inevitably lead to a much worse Y, without showing how the slide actually happens. “If we permit medical aid in dying for the terminally ill, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”


Ad Hominem Attacking the person making the argument rather than the argument itself. “You can’t take Peter Singer seriously on animal ethics — he holds a lot of unpopular views.”


Naturalistic Fallacy Inferring an ought directly from an is — that what happens in nature shows what should happen morally. “Humans evolved as omnivores, therefore eating meat is morally permissible.”


False Dichotomy Framing a complex issue as if there were only two options, when others exist. “Either we let the market ration health care, or the system will collapse.”


Appeal to Authority Treating the testimony of an authority as decisive even when they are speaking outside their expertise — or when other experts disagree. “My favorite podcaster says vaccines cause autism, and he has done a lot of research.”

His own stated wishes, formed over a long life?
His daughter’s fear of losing him?

Beneficence is not the same as paternalism. It is the discipline of finding out what this person counts as a life worth living — and acting in service of that.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Non-Maleficence in Practice

Non-Maleficence do no harm


The case. A new chemotherapy regimen offers a 30% chance of remission and a 5% chance of fatal cardiotoxicity. Standard care offers neither.
The principle does not say “never harm.” It asks:

Is the expected harm proportionate to the expected benefit?
Does the patient understand both sides clearly enough to choose?

Every IV start is a small puncture wound. Every medication has side effects. The principle’s job is to forbid hidden, gratuitous, or disproportionate risk — not all risk.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Justice in Practice

Justice treat fairly


The case. Two 55-year-olds arrive in the ED with chest pain and no obvious red flags. One is on Medicaid; the other has private insurance. Decades of research show that, on average, they will not get the same workup.
Justice at the bedside is rarely a kidney allocation decision. It looks like:

Are we ordering tests for clinical reasons — or for who the patient appears to be?
Are our floors and ICUs staffed and supplied equitably?
Where do we, as nurses, push back against disparate care?

Justice asks us to look up from the individual patient and notice the pattern.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
What Is a Fallacy?
A fallacy is an error in reasoning — an argument that looks like good support but isn’t.

Not the same as a lie. The speaker may sincerely believe it.
A fallacious argument can still have a true conclusion — the defect is in the structure, not the conclusion.
Most bioethics fallacies are informal: the form looks fine, but the content misleads through irrelevance, ambiguity, or hidden assumption.

The next two slides cover the six you will see most often.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Common Fallacies in Bioethics


Appeal to Nature Inferring that something is good (or bad) merely because it is natural (or unnatural). “IVF lets older women conceive — but it’s unnatural, so it must be wrong.”


Slippery Slope Predicting that allowing X will inevitably lead to a much worse Y, without showing how the slide actually happens. “If we permit medical aid in dying for the terminally ill, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”


Ad Hominem Attacking the person making the argument rather than the argument itself. “You can’t take Peter Singer seriously on animal ethics — he holds a lot of unpopular views.”


Naturalistic Fallacy Inferring an ought directly from an is — that what happens in nature shows what should happen morally. “Humans evolved as omnivores, therefore eating meat is morally permissible.”


False Dichotomy Framing a complex issue as if there were only two options, when others exist. “Either we let the market ration health care, or the system will collapse.”


Appeal to Authority Treating the testimony of an authority as decisive even when they are speaking outside their expertise — or when other experts disagree. “My favorite podcaster says vaccines cause autism, and he has done a lot of research.”

3.6 After You’ve Discussed

Where the duty actually lands. Most U.S. conscience-clause laws place the duty to still care for the patient on the institution, not on the individual clinician. That shifts the question from “can she refuse?” to “what does the institution owe the patient when she does?”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.13 After You’ve Discussed






Note



How the principles typically map.

The case is often framed as Beneficence and Non-Maleficence pulling toward transfusion, Autonomy pulling against.
But notice the twist: respecting her refusal may itself be the most beneficent act, and transfusing a competent patient against her will is itself a form of harm.
The principles do not decide the case. They give us shared language to argue about it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
1.1 What Theories Do
The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary. But they do not, by themselves, tell us which principle to prioritize when they conflict (Beauchamp and Childress 2019).
Ethical theories fill that gap. Think of them as lenses:

Each focuses on something real
Each has blind spots
No single lens sees everything

We will use all four throughout the course — not to pick a winner, but to see more of the problem.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
1.2 A Roadmap








Theory
Core question
Key figure




Utilitarianism
What produces the best outcomes?
Mill, Bentham


Kantian ethics
What are my duties, regardless of outcome?
Kant


Virtue ethics
What would a person of good character do?
Aristotle


Care ethics
What do my relationships and context require?
Gilligan, Held




Remind students: these will appear in almost every subsequent lecture. Abortion, end of life, justice — each one sees these theories pulling differently.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.1 Mill and Bentham




J. S. Mill, c. 1870. London Stereoscopic Co. Public domain.




Jeremy Bentham, by H. W. Pickersgill. Public domain.




“Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness; wrong as they tend to produce the reverse of happiness.” — Mill (Mill 1863)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.2 The Greatest Happiness Principle
Utilitarianism judges actions entirely by their consequences:

What matters is welfare — happiness, well-being, the absence of suffering
All individuals count equally; no one gets extra weight
The morally required action is the one that maximizes total welfare

Bentham called this the hedonic calculus — weigh the pleasures and pains produced, add them up, choose the highest score (Bentham 1789).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.3 Utilitarianism and the Four Principles
Beneficence is central — acting to maximize welfare just is acting beneficently.
Justice emerges from impartiality: each person counts as one, so unfair distributions that reduce total welfare are wrong.
Autonomy is valued instrumentally — respecting autonomy usually maximizes welfare — but can be overridden when it doesn’t.
Non-Maleficence is captured under the harm side of the ledger.






Note



The tension. A strict utilitarian could justify deceiving a patient if the deception produces better outcomes. Most clinicians resist this — which is why other theories matter.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.5 Thought Question

A city has 10 ventilators and 15 patients who need one to survive. Five of the fifteen have a much higher probability of surviving with ventilator support.
A utilitarian protocol would allocate ventilators to maximize lives saved — which likely means the five highest-probability patients get priority.

Is that fair to the other ten patients?
Does it matter why some patients have lower survival odds — age, disability, underlying illness?
What would the principle of justice say that utilitarianism might miss?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



The utilitarian reply. A sophisticated utilitarian can say: the protocol maximizes welfare across the group, which is what justice requires. The critique is that it treats some lives as worth less, and that the reasons behind lower survival odds (disability, chronic illness) are often tied to existing inequities. We will return to this in L9 (Public Health Ethics) and L8 (Justice).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Kant




Immanuel Kant, painted portrait. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).


Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that morality is grounded in reason, not consequences.
The key test is his Categorical Imperative — a rule for whether an action is morally permissible, applied before you look at outcomes.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 The Humanity Formulation
The most clinically relevant form of the Categorical Imperative (Kant 1785):

“Act so that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in that of another, always as an end and never as a means only.”

In practice, this means:

Persons have dignity — an unconditional worth that cannot be traded off
Deceiving, manipulating, or coercing a patient uses them as a mere means
Informed consent is not just policy — it is a moral requirement

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 3.3 Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
3.3 Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is Kant’s central principle — respecting autonomy is what it means to treat persons as ends.
Non-Maleficence reflects the duty not to harm persons who have inherent dignity.
Beneficence is a genuine duty, but cannot override autonomy — benefiting someone by deceiving them is still wrong.
Justice flows from treating persons equally as ends — no one’s dignity is worth more than another’s.






Note



Contrast with utilitarianism. Kant would say: you must never lie to the Jehovah’s Witness patient about her prognosis to get her to consent, even if lying would save her life.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.5 Thought Question

A patient with severe depression has been refusing an antidepressant. His psychiatrist suspects the refusal is itself a symptom of the illness — that his depression is preventing him from appreciating how treatment could help him.

Does the Kantian view help here — or complicate it?
When, if ever, does treating the illness require overriding the patient’s expressed wishes?
How is this different from the Jehovah’s Witness case?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



The Kantian tension. Kant’s framework requires that the patient be capable of rational self-governance for autonomy to apply fully. If the illness itself impairs that capacity, overriding expressed wishes may be consistent with treating him as an end — but this reasoning can also be abused to justify paternalism. Decision-making capacity assessment (L2) is how clinical ethics tries to hold this line carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.1 Aristotle and Eudaimonia




Aristotle (copy of Lysippos bust). Palazzo Altemps. Public domain.


Aristotle asked not “what should I do?” but “what kind of person should I be?” (Aristotle 340 BC)
Eudaimonia — often translated flourishing — is the goal of human life: living and acting in accordance with the virtues of a fully developed human being.
A virtue is a stable character trait — developed through practice — that reliably disposes a person to act and feel well.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 4.2 The Virtues of the Good Nurse
text:
4.2 The Virtues of the Good Nurse
Virtue ethics is especially useful in professional ethics, because professions define role-specific excellences.
The ANA Code of Ethics (American Nurses Association 2015) describes the good nurse as someone who embodies:

Compassion — genuine concern for the patient’s suffering
Integrity — consistency between values and actions, even under pressure
Fidelity — keeping commitments made to patients and profession
Practical wisdom (phronesis) — knowing how to apply general principles to particular situations

These are not policies. They are traits you develop by practicing them.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 4.3 Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
4.3 Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
Virtue ethics asks: what would a person of good character do in this situation?
Beneficence — the virtuous clinician genuinely cares about the patient’s good, not just follows protocols.
Non-Maleficence — integrity means honesty about what you can and cannot do.
Autonomy — respect for persons flows from compassion and fidelity, not just rules.
Justice — fairness requires the virtue of impartiality even toward patients you find difficult.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.1 Gilligan and the Ethics of Care
text:
5.1 Gilligan and the Ethics of Care
Carol Gilligan (Gilligan 1982) argued that dominant moral theories — utilitarian and Kantian — were built around abstract rules applied to strangers. They missed what actually structures most moral life: relationships, dependency, and the responsibility that comes with them.

“The moral problem arises from conflicting responsibilities rather than competing rights and requires for its resolution a mode of thinking that is contextual and narrative rather than formal and abstract.” — Gilligan, In a Different Voice (1982)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.2 Relationships, Context, Vulnerability
Care ethics, developed by Gilligan and later Virginia Held (Held 2006), asks:

Who is this patient, in this situation, with this set of relationships?
What do I owe them given the relationship I have with them?
Whose needs are being made invisible by abstract rule-following?

In nursing practice, care ethics is not a soft add-on. It describes what nurses already know: the moral weight of the bedside relationship cannot be reduced to a checklist.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.3 Care Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
5.3 Care Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is reframed: rather than the isolated individual making a formal choice, it is the relational self — embedded in family, community, culture.
Beneficence is particularized: it matters what this person needs, not what the average patient needs.
Non-Maleficence includes attending to emotional harm and the harm of not being seen or heard.
Justice requires attending to who is made vulnerable by social structures — and who gets left out of abstract policy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.5 Thought Question

A 78-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment makes decisions that worry her adult children. She insists on living alone; she sometimes forgets meals; she declines the assisted living facility they have arranged.

A utilitarian might look at her risk profile and long-term welfare. A Kantian might ask about her decision-making capacity. A virtue ethicist might ask what a caring daughter or a virtuous clinician would do.
What does the care ethics lens add that the others miss?
Is there anything it might obscure?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



What care ethics adds. It centers the relationship and the patient’s embeddedness in family and community — her story, not just her risk score. What it can obscure: the caring family can also be a controlling one; care ethics needs to ask whether the relationships are themselves just. This is one reason the four principles framework is used alongside the theories rather than replaced by any single one.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.1 The Theories as Lenses








Theory
Foundational commitment
Role of the four principles




Utilitarianism
Maximize welfare
Principles are derived — follow them because doing so usually produces the best outcomes


Kantian ethics
Respect persons as ends
Principles are duties grounded in rational dignity — especially autonomy


Virtue ethics
Cultivate good character
Principles are expressions of virtue — practical wisdom tells you how to apply them


Care ethics
Honor relationships
Principles are contextualized by relationship and dependency — not applied abstractly

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.2 Which Theory Is Right?
Probably none of them — completely. Each illuminates something the others miss:

Utilitarianism sees the population and the aggregate.
Kantian ethics sees the dignity of the individual that cannot be traded away.
Virtue ethics sees the character of the clinician and the quality of the relationship.
Care ethics sees the context and dependency that abstract rules can erase.

The four principles work in part because they are theory-agnostic: a Kantian and a utilitarian can both endorse autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice — they just disagree about why and how to weight them.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.3 Wrapping Up Lecture 1

Bioethics uses a four-principle framework as its shared vocabulary, supported by four major ethical theories that offer different answers to the question of how to weigh those principles. Utilitarianism focuses on outcomes and aggregate welfare. Kantian ethics focuses on duties and the dignity of persons. Virtue ethics focuses on character. Care ethics focuses on relationships and context. No single theory is correct; used as lenses, they let us see more of what is morally at stake in any clinical situation.
Lecture 2 turns to the history that gave these principles their urgency, and the clinical relationship where they are daily tested.

Relationships, Context, Vulnerability
Care ethics, developed by Gilligan and later Virginia Held (Held 2006), asks:

Who is this patient, in this situation, with this set of relationships?
What do I owe them given the relationship I have with them?
Whose needs are being made invisible by abstract rule-following?

In nursing practice, care ethics is not a soft add-on. It describes what nurses already know: the moral weight of the bedside relationship cannot be reduced to a checklist.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Care Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
Care Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is reframed: rather than the isolated individual making a formal choice, it is the relational self — embedded in family, community, culture.
Beneficence is particularized: it matters what this person needs, not what the average patient needs.
Non-Maleficence includes attending to emotional harm and the harm of not being seen or heard.
Justice requires attending to who is made vulnerable by social structures — and who gets left out of abstract policy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

A 78-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment makes decisions that worry her adult children. She insists on living alone; she sometimes forgets meals; she declines the assisted living facility they have arranged.

A utilitarian might look at her risk profile and long-term welfare. A Kantian might ask about her decision-making capacity. A virtue ethicist might ask what a caring daughter or a virtuous clinician would do.
What does the care ethics lens add that the others miss?
Is there anything it might obscure?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


What care ethics adds. It centers the relationship and the patient’s embeddedness in family and community — her story, not just her risk score. What it can obscure: the caring family can also be a controlling one; care ethics needs to ask whether the relationships are themselves just. This is one reason the four principles framework is used alongside the theories rather than replaced by any single one.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Theories as Lenses








Theory
Foundational commitment
Role of the four principles




Utilitarianism
Maximize welfare
Principles are derived — follow them because doing so usually produces the best outcomes


Kantian ethics
Respect persons as ends
Principles are duties grounded in rational dignity — especially autonomy


Virtue ethics
Cultivate good character
Principles are expressions of virtue — practical wisdom tells you how to apply them


Care ethics
Honor relationships
Principles are contextualized by relationship and dependency — not applied abstractly

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Which Theory Is Right?
Probably none of them — completely. Each illuminates something the others miss:

Utilitarianism sees the population and the aggregate.
Kantian ethics sees the dignity of the individual that cannot be traded away.
Virtue ethics sees the character of the clinician and the quality of the relationship.
Care ethics sees the context and dependency that abstract rules can erase.

The four principles work in part because they are theory-agnostic: a Kantian and a utilitarian can both endorse autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice — they just disagree about why and how to weight them.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Wrapping Up Lecture 1

Bioethics uses a four-principle framework as its shared vocabulary, supported by four major ethical theories that offer different answers to the question of how to weigh those principles. Utilitarianism focuses on outcomes and aggregate welfare. Kantian ethics focuses on duties and the dignity of persons. Virtue ethics focuses on character. Care ethics focuses on relationships and context. No single theory is correct; used as lenses, they let us see more of what is morally at stake in any clinical situation.
Lecture 2 turns to the history that gave these principles their urgency, and the clinical relationship where they are daily tested.

util


Q

Which option produces
better expected outcomes?



A

Drug A
(aggressive)



Q->A





B

Drug B
(conservative)



Q->B





EA

Recovery: likely, benefit large
Side effects: unlikely, harm serious
→ Weighted benefit: HIGH



A->EA





EB

Recovery: possible, benefit moderate
Side effects: likely, harm minor
→ Weighted benefit: MODERATE



B->EB





R

Prescribe Drug A
(higher weighted benefit)



EA->R





EB->R






 Utilitarian decision procedure: for each option, estimate the probability and magnitude of every likely outcome (benefits and harms), sum the weighted results, and choose the option with the highest overall expected benefit.
